headline: Netflix Would Have Crashed on Super Bowl Sunday — If Not For This
subheadline: The invisible system keeping the internet alive under pressure

lesson_pages.title: Your App Just Got Featured on TV. Now What?
lesson_pages.body: Traffic just spiked 50x. Without Auto Scaling, every request after your server maxes out returns an error. Your launch becomes a disaster. Auto Scaling spins up new EC2 instances automatically — before users even notice the load.

lesson_pages.title: Three Numbers. One Safety Net.
lesson_pages.body: Min = the floor. Your app never drops below this. Max = the ceiling. You never get surprise-billed beyond this. Desired = where AWS tries to keep you right now. These three numbers are the difference between a smooth launch and a 3am crisis.

lesson_pages.title: It's Watching Everything.
lesson_pages.body: CPU over 70%? Scale out. CPU under 30%? Scale in. Queue too deep? Scale out. Too many 5xx errors? Scale out. Auto Scaling reacts to whatever metric you care about. You set the triggers. It pulls the levers.

headline: Businesses Are Paying 90% Too Much for Cloud Storage — Here's the Fix
subheadline: The tiered system most AWS users never touch

lesson_pages.title: You're Probably Paying for Storage You Never Touch
lesson_pages.body: S3 Standard costs ~$23/TB/month. Glacier costs ~$4. If you're storing 5-year-old compliance logs in Standard, you're lighting money on fire. AWS has 6 storage tiers. Most teams use 1.

lesson_pages.title: The Tiers, in Plain English
lesson_pages.body: Standard = hit it daily. Standard-IA = hit it monthly (cheaper, but a retrieval fee). Glacier Instant = hit it quarterly. Glacier Flexible = hit it once a year (minutes to retrieve). Deep Archive = open once a decade. Pick wrong and you pay twice — storage + retrieval.

lesson_pages.title: Let AWS Figure It Out For You
lesson_pages.body: S3 Intelligent-Tiering watches your access patterns and moves objects automatically. You pay a tiny monitoring fee. In return, objects no one's touched in 30 days quietly drop to cheaper tiers. Zero effort. Real savings.

headline: One Misconfigured Policy Exposed 100 Million Credit Card Numbers
subheadline: The Capital One breach of 2019 came down to a single IAM role

lesson_pages.title: The Breach That Cost $80 Million
lesson_pages.body: Capital One's 2019 breach wasn't a sophisticated hack. A misconfigured IAM role let a compromised EC2 instance query S3 buckets it had no business touching. 106 million records leaked. One over-permissioned role. That's all it took.

lesson_pages.title: Who Gets What Access
lesson_pages.body: Users = real humans logging in. Groups = give permissions to 50 people at once. Roles = temporary identities for services. Your EC2 instance should never have your personal credentials hardcoded in it. Give it a role. That role has exactly the permissions it needs — nothing else.

lesson_pages.title: Deny Always Wins. Use That.
lesson_pages.body: AWS defaults to deny everything. Every permission you grant is explicit. If a policy allows S3 access and another denies it, Deny wins. Always. Build your security model around this: grant narrow permissions, add explicit denies for anything critical. This is the principle of least privilege. It's not optional.

headline: The Invisible Wall Around Your Cloud That Most People Set Up Wrong
subheadline: VPCs are why a compromised server doesn't become a full breach

lesson_pages.title: Without This, Everything Is Public
lesson_pages.body: By default, AWS resources can talk to each other and sometimes to the internet. A VPC puts everything inside a private network you control. If an attacker compromises one server, the VPC stops them from jumping to your database. It's the wall between 'incident' and 'catastrophe.'

lesson_pages.title: Public Subnets Are for the Front Door Only
lesson_pages.body: Your load balancer lives in a public subnet. Your database never does. Private subnets have no route to the internet — they can only be reached from inside your VPC. If your RDS instance has a public IP, fix that right now.

lesson_pages.title: Two Locks, Not One
lesson_pages.body: Security Groups are per-instance firewalls. Stateful — if you allowed a request in, the response gets out automatically. NACLs are per-subnet. Stateless — you must explicitly allow both directions. Defense-in-depth means using both. One being misconfigured shouldn't be the whole story.

headline: The Wrong Load Balancer Type Just Cost This Company Its Launch
subheadline: ALB, NLB, GLB — three tools for three completely different problems

lesson_pages.title: Picking the Wrong One Is Expensive to Fix
lesson_pages.body: You built your microservices routing around NLB. Now you need path-based routing and it doesn't support it. Rearchitecting under launch pressure. This happens. ALB vs NLB isn't a minor detail — it determines what your entire traffic layer can do.

lesson_pages.title: ALB: The Smart One
lesson_pages.body: Reads HTTP headers. Routes /api to one service, /admin to another. Understands hostnames, cookies, query strings. Use ALB for web apps, microservices, containers. It's not the fastest — but it's the most intelligent.

lesson_pages.title: NLB: The Fast One
lesson_pages.body: Doesn't care what's in the packet. Just moves TCP/UDP as fast as possible. Millions of requests per second, single-digit millisecond latency. Use NLB when you need raw speed, static IPs, or non-HTTP protocols. The exam tests you on this distinction every time.

headline: SQL vs NoSQL: The Database Decision That Bankrupted a Startup
subheadline: Picking the wrong database at scale means rewriting everything

lesson_pages.title: The Pivot That Killed Them
lesson_pages.body: They built on PostgreSQL. Growth hit. Complex JOIN queries at 100k users became timeouts at 10 million. Rewriting to DynamoDB while keeping the product running nearly killed the company. The database decision you make at month one follows you for years.

lesson_pages.title: RDS: When Your Data Has Relationships
lesson_pages.body: Orders belong to customers. Customers have addresses. Items have categories. That's relational data — use RDS. MySQL, PostgreSQL, Oracle, SQL Server. Complex queries, ACID transactions, strict schemas. Scales vertically. Struggles with unstructured, unpredictable data.

lesson_pages.title: DynamoDB: When Scale Is Non-Negotiable
lesson_pages.body: Amazon's shopping cart runs on DynamoDB. Millions of reads per second. Single-digit millisecond response. No schema — store whatever shape you need. There are no joins though. If your query requires joining two tables, DynamoDB will punish you. Know your access patterns before you choose.

headline: The Server That Doesn't Exist — and Bills You $0 When Nothing Happens
subheadline: Serverless changed cloud economics forever. Here's why.

lesson_pages.title: You're Paying for Idle Servers Right Now
lesson_pages.body: That EC2 instance running at 3% CPU for 22 hours a day — you're paying for all 24. Lambda flips this. You pay per 1ms of actual execution. 1 million requests per month free. For low-traffic workloads, Lambda costs practically nothing.

lesson_pages.title: It Runs on Everything
lesson_pages.body: File uploaded to S3? Lambda runs. HTTP request hits API Gateway? Lambda runs. Message dropped in SQS? Lambda runs. DynamoDB record changes? Lambda runs. Schedule hits midnight? Lambda runs. It's the backbone of every serverless architecture because it connects to everything.

lesson_pages.title: The Limits That Will Bite You
lesson_pages.body: 15 minute max runtime. 10GB max memory. 50MB deployment package. First invocation after idle = cold start latency. For long-running tasks, use Step Functions. For heavy compute, use Fargate. For cold start sensitivity, use Provisioned Concurrency. Know the ceiling before you hit it.

headline: How Your Website Loads in 50ms From Any Country on Earth
subheadline: CloudFront's 450+ edge locations make distance irrelevant

lesson_pages.title: Physics Is Your Enemy. CloudFront Is Your Fix.
lesson_pages.body: Light travels ~200ms round trip from New York to Sydney. That's the floor — and your actual requests are slower. CloudFront caches your content at 450+ edge locations worldwide. A user in Sydney hits an edge 10ms away, not your origin 200ms away. You can't beat physics. You can cache around it.

lesson_pages.title: One Distribution, Multiple Origins
lesson_pages.body: Serve /api/* from your ALB in us-east-1. Serve /images/* from S3. Serve /video/* from a media server. All through one CloudFront distribution. Users get a single fast endpoint. Your architecture stays complex behind the scenes without users ever noticing.

lesson_pages.title: Security Comes for Free
lesson_pages.body: WAF blocks SQL injection and XSS at the edge — before the request costs you any compute. Geo-restriction blocks entire countries in two clicks. Signed URLs mean only paying customers see your premium content. Shield Standard absorbs DDoS automatically. You get all of this without touching your origin.

headline: The DNS Service That Rerouted 50 Million Users During a Data Center Fire
subheadline: Route 53 does more than resolve domain names

lesson_pages.title: DNS Is How the Internet Finds You. Route 53 Is How You Survive Disasters.
lesson_pages.body: When us-east-1 goes down — and it has — companies with Route 53 failover routing automatically rerouted traffic to us-west-2. Companies without it showed an error page for hours. DNS is the first decision traffic makes. Control it.

lesson_pages.title: Six Routing Policies. One Exam.
lesson_pages.body: Simple = one IP. Weighted = send 10% to the canary. Latency = send users to the fastest region. Failover = primary dies, secondary takes over. Geolocation = EU users hit EU servers. Geoproximity = fine-grained geographic control with a bias dial. The exam gives you a scenario and you pick the right one.

lesson_pages.title: Health Checks Are the Autopilot
lesson_pages.body: Route 53 pings your endpoints every 30 seconds. Three failures in a row and it stops sending traffic there — automatically. No PagerDuty. No manual DNS update. No 3am phone call. You wake up, your backup region handled everything. Set these up for every critical endpoint.

headline: Two AWS Messaging Services — One Costly Architectural Mistake
subheadline: Most teams pick one when they needed both

lesson_pages.title: Direct Service Calls Are a Ticking Bomb
lesson_pages.body: Order service directly calls billing, inventory, and shipping. Billing is down for 10 minutes. Now orders are failing. Customers are screaming. SQS exists to prevent this. Drop the message in a queue. Billing processes it when it comes back up. Nothing lost. No cascading failure.

lesson_pages.title: SQS Queues. SNS Broadcasts.
lesson_pages.body: SQS: one message, one consumer pulls it. Sits in queue until processed. Retries automatically on failure. Perfect for background jobs and decoupling services. SNS: one message, every subscriber gets it instantly. Perfect for notifications and triggering multiple systems at once. They solve different problems.

lesson_pages.title: Fan-Out: When You Need Both
lesson_pages.body: Order placed → publish to SNS topic → billing queue gets it, inventory queue gets it, shipping queue gets it. Three independent consumers. If one falls behind, the others don't care. This is the exam's favorite architecture. It's also how Amazon actually works.

headline: Three AWS Storage Types — Pick Wrong and Rebuild Your Architecture
subheadline: Block, file, and object storage are not interchangeable

lesson_pages.title: These Are Not the Same Thing
lesson_pages.body: Trying to mount S3 like a hard drive doesn't work. Trying to attach EFS to a database creates a performance disaster. Each of the three storage types exists for a specific job. Using the wrong one means rebuilding. The exam will test whether you know which is which.

lesson_pages.title: EBS: Your Instance's Hard Drive
lesson_pages.body: Block storage. One instance, one volume. Low latency, high IOPS. Lives in one AZ — if your instance moves, so does the data problem. Use it for your OS, databases, anything needing raw disk speed. Don't share it between instances.

lesson_pages.title: EFS vs S3: Shared Files vs Unlimited Objects
lesson_pages.body: EFS is a network file system. Mount it on 100 EC2 instances simultaneously. All see the same files. Multi-AZ. More expensive than S3. Use it when multiple servers need shared file access. S3 is accessed via API — not a mounted drive. Unlimited scale, cheapest at volume. Use it for backups, assets, data lakes. Don't use either one where the other belongs.

headline: Amazon Built a Database That Survives AZ Failures Without You Noticing
subheadline: Aurora isn't just RDS — it's a fundamentally different architecture

lesson_pages.title: Standard RDS Fails Over. Aurora Just Keeps Going.
lesson_pages.body: RDS Multi-AZ has a standby that takes 30-60 seconds to promote when primary dies. Your app gets errors during that window. Aurora's storage is replicated 6 ways across 3 AZs automatically. Lose an AZ and Aurora doesn't blink. The failover you never noticed happened.

lesson_pages.title: Faster, More Replicas, Less Drama
lesson_pages.body: 5x faster than MySQL, 3x faster than PostgreSQL. 15 read replicas vs RDS's 5. Aurora Serverless scales compute up and down automatically — pay for what you use, nothing more. This isn't incremental improvement. It's a different product that happens to speak MySQL and PostgreSQL.

lesson_pages.title: The Multi-Region Trick That Saves Companies
lesson_pages.body: Aurora Global Database replicates to a second region with under 1 second lag. us-east-1 goes down? Promote eu-west-1 in under a minute. Route 53 flips DNS. Users barely notice. This is the exam's correct answer for 'sub-second RPO, sub-minute RTO, multi-region.' Remember it.

headline: The Lock Protecting Trillions in Financial Data — and How AWS Implements It
subheadline: Encryption at rest vs in transit: two different problems, two different solutions

lesson_pages.title: One Without the Other Is Useless
lesson_pages.body: Encrypting your database at rest but sending it over plain HTTP? An attacker intercepts it in transit — encryption at rest means nothing. Encrypting in transit but leaving the database unencrypted? Physical access to the storage = all your data. You need both. Always both.

lesson_pages.title: KMS: The Key to the Keys
lesson_pages.body: Your master encryption key lives in KMS. You never see it in plaintext. S3, EBS, RDS, DynamoDB — they all call KMS to encrypt and decrypt. You control who can use which keys via IAM. Customer Managed Keys let you rotate, disable, and audit usage. AWS Managed Keys are simpler but you control less.

lesson_pages.title: Why Envelope Encryption Exists
lesson_pages.body: You can't encrypt 100GB with a KMS key directly — too slow, too expensive. Instead: KMS generates a data key, data key encrypts your 100GB, KMS key encrypts the data key. That tiny encrypted data key gets stored alongside your data. To decrypt: call KMS once to unwrap the data key, then decrypt locally. One API call. Terabytes of data protected.

headline: Multi-AZ vs Multi-Region: A Half-Million Dollar Architecture Decision
subheadline: Understanding RTO and RPO is the difference between 99.9% and 99.999% uptime

lesson_pages.title: What You're Actually Defending Against
lesson_pages.body: Multi-AZ protects against a single data center failing — power outage, network issue, hardware failure. Multi-Region protects against an entire AWS region going down — which has happened. The more you want to protect against, the more you spend. The exam gives you an outage scenario and makes you decide.

lesson_pages.title: RTO and RPO Are the Only Numbers That Matter
lesson_pages.body: RTO: how long until you're back online. RPO: how much data you're willing to lose. RTO of 4 hours? Backup and restore works fine. RTO of 5 minutes? You need something already running. RPO of zero? You need synchronous replication. These aren't architectural preferences — they're business requirements that determine your entire DR strategy.

lesson_pages.title: Four Tiers. Pick Based on Cost, Not Fear.
lesson_pages.body: Backup & Restore: hours of RTO, cheapest. Pilot Light: critical infra running warm, minutes. Warm Standby: full copy at reduced capacity, minutes. Multi-Site Active-Active: instant, most expensive. Every step up is a real cost jump. The exam gives you an RTO/RPO requirement. You match it to the cheapest tier that still meets the bar.

headline: The AWS Bill That Went From $800 to $80,000 in One Weekend
subheadline: Every $80K AWS horror story has the same root causes

lesson_pages.title: The Bill Horror Story Is Always the Same
lesson_pages.body: Forgot to delete an EC2 instance. Left a NAT Gateway running in dev. Enabled a feature that charges per GB without reading the pricing page. AWS will charge you for everything you leave running. No mercy. No automatic shutoff. Budget alerts and AWS Cost Explorer are not optional — they're survival tools.

lesson_pages.title: On-Demand Is the Most Expensive Way to Run AWS
lesson_pages.body: On-Demand: full price, no commitment. Reserved Instances: commit to 1 or 3 years, save up to 75%. Spot: use AWS's spare capacity, save up to 90% — but it can be yanked back with 2 minutes notice. The right answer: Reserved for your steady baseline, Spot for batch jobs that can be interrupted, On-Demand only for unpredictable peaks.

lesson_pages.title: Where the Hidden Costs Live
lesson_pages.body: Data transfer out to the internet costs money. Every GB. NAT Gateway charges per GB processed. EBS volumes keep billing after you terminate the instance — unless you delete them. Elastic IPs cost money when not attached. Snapshots accumulate silently. Use Trusted Advisor and Cost Explorer to hunt these down. The biggest AWS bills usually aren't from running the obvious things too much.
